summary: The post explains how to set up a reverse VPN to securely expose services from a home network to the public internet, rather than the traditional use of VPNs for accessing office networks remotely. Jess walks through the complete Docker-based setup using OpenVPN, from generating certificates on a cloud instance to configuring the client on a home NAS. The key insight is using Docker's network namespace sharing to route specific containers through the VPN tunnel, giving fine-grained control over what gets exposed.
key_points: Reverse VPNs flip the traditional model - instead of accessing private networks from outside, they expose home services to the public
Using Docker containers for both the VPN server and client provides clean isolation and easy deployment
The --net container:openvpn flag lets you selectively route only specific containers through the VPN tunnel
This setup is useful for home automation webhooks, media servers, and other services that need public accessibility
money_quotes: A reverse VPN is for exposing things from your home network into the public. Why? Well for one, you shouldn't want to expose your home network to the world. There are a lot of risks in doing that. A reverse VPN allows you to securely control what you are exposing.
Of course on my NAS, I have docker installed, but you can install openvpn on your host too (but I will judge you).
Now all you have to do it run all your other containers in --net container:openvpn!
Alternatively if you would rather run everything on your server over the vpn you can run the openvpn container with --net host. I personally like the control though.
key_insight: Docker's network namespace sharing combined with a reverse VPN creates a secure, granular way to expose specific home services to the internet without opening up your entire network.
